coredata.dc:title: An Aflatoxin-M1 biochip using graphene quantum dot-gold hybrid nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Ramalingam, Saipriya
Elsayed, Abdallah
Singh, Ashutosh
coredata.dc:description: Toxicosis through food and feed has remained a point of concern to food sectors across the globe. Although strict regulations have been implemented in developed and developing countries, their detection in food matrices is an evolving science. This study focuses on the development and fabrication of an electrochemical microfluidic biosensor that deploys aptamers to detect trace concentrations of Aflatoxin-M1 (AF-M1) in milk samples. The use of graphene quantum dot composite with Au nanoparticles anchors the aptamer to the sensor surface and improves its signal conductivity. The screen-printed carbon electrode modified with graphene quantum dot-gold nanoparticles is placed between two polydimethylsiloxane layers to promote portability and improve mixing effects. Differential pulse voltammograms indicated that the linear range of the sensor was between 100 pM and 2 nM making the limit of detection 0.3 nM. Interfering molecules of similar size were analyzed, to evaluate sensor selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin-M1
0D nanocomposites
Graphene quantum dot-gold
Electrochemical sensor
Microfluidic biochip

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs - U of G Partnership

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors sincerely to express their gratitude to the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada for funding this study (Grant # RGPIN-2017-03975). The authors would also like to acknowledge the Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs - U of G Partnership for funding this study through their Highly Qualified Personnel Scholarship Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (i) Schematic representation of the modification of sensor surface using GQDs and an aptamer sequence specific to AF-M1 (ii)TEM of: (a) GQD nanoparticles inset: histogram of GQD distribution in sample space;(b) GQDA nanoparticles inset: histogram of GQD distribution, (c) magnified image of GQDA nanoparticles indicating core–shell structure.

a) SEM/EDX maps of (a) GQD and (b) GQDA samples; c) Visible spectrum of GQD and GQDA (d) Photoluminescence data for GQD and GQDA samples upon providing a 340 nm excitation wavelength; (e) FTIR peaks showing distinct vibrational modes for GQD and GQDA samples (f) Raman spectra indicating D and G bands for the synthesized GQD and GQDA.

(a) Electrodeposition of GQDA on the WE surface of the screen-printed carbon electrode (b) Cyclic voltammograms of Bare, GQD and GQDA modified electrode surfaces (c) Scan- rate dependent studies for GQDA modified electrodes from 10 to 150 mV/s d) Impedance spectra of (i) Bare electrode (ii) GQD and (iii) GQDA. Inset: equivalent circuit modeled over 9 iterations [R s(C dl[R ct Z W])].

(a) Optimization of (i) aptamer concentration and (ii) incubation time; (b) Photograph of the fabricated continuous flow PDMS biochip containing the screen-printed carbon electrode between the layers. Sections of the chip are shown as seen under 20X magnification; (c) Differential pulse voltammograms obtained upon the introduction of increasing concentrations of AF-M1 onto the GQDA- modified electrode (d) extrapolated peak currents to deduce the linear range of the sensor.
Interference study performed on the AF-M1 aptamer modified electrode using Okadaic Acid, Brevetoxin, bovine serum albumin, hen-egg lysozyme and AF-M1 taken in equal concentrations.
(a): Spike – analysis performed on biochip using milk samples; (b) Sensor validation using AF-M1 ELISA Kit.

head.ce:title: An Aflatoxin-M1 biochip using graphene quantum dot-gold hybrid nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, University of Guelph, Guelph, ON N1G 2W1, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, University of Guelph, Guelph, ON N1G 2W1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Toxicosis through food and feed has remained a point of concern to food sectors across the globe. Although strict regulations have been implemented in developed and developing countries, their detection in food matrices is an evolving science. This study focuses on the development and fabrication of an electrochemical microfluidic biosensor that deploys aptamers to detect trace concentrations of Aflatoxin-M1 (AF-M1) in milk samples. The use of graphene quantum dot composite with Au nanoparticles anchors the aptamer to the sensor surface and improves its signal conductivity. The screen-printed carbon electrode modified with graphene quantum dot-gold nanoparticles is placed between two polydimethylsiloxane layers to promote portability and improve mixing effects. Differential pulse voltammograms indicated that the linear range of the sensor was between 100 pM and 2 nM making the limit of detection 0.3 nM. Interfering molecules of similar size were analyzed, to evaluate sensor selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Milk is one of the most wholesome foods ever known to humans and essential in maintaining a well-balanced diet. Despite the emergence of vegan alternatives, cow milk remains a popular dairy product across the globe. The EU and India remain top consumers in the current global milk market (Handford et al., 2016). With per-capita production on the rise to meet increasing demand, there remains a pressing need to monitor the quality of milk that is being produced and distributed.

Mycotoxins in food have always remained a serious health concern for not only humans but also ruminants such as cows, goats, and pigs. While research suggests that the animal’s rumen can act as a natural barrier against mycotoxins, other studies have shown that toxins from contaminated feed can remain in the body of the animal and be transferred to the milk they produce (IARC, 2012). Of the many contaminants present in milk, aflatoxins have garnered attention due to their acute toxicological effects. Aflatoxins are a group of cyclic coumarin ring structures that are produced by two major fungal species, Aspergillus parasiticus, and Aspergillus flavus. The most common chemical structures of the most aflatoxins present in nature are shown in Fig. S1. Although aflatoxigenic fungi widely contaminate agricultural staples, Aflatoxin M1 (AF-M1) in bovine milk, is known to pose a higher health hazard in humans upon consumption (Sharafi et al., 2022). When Aflatoxin B1 (AF-B1) potent feed is ingested, it undergoes several species-specific metabolic conversions. In dairy cows, ruminant microbes in the mouth convert some of the AF-B1 to aflatoxicol, which is transported through the bloodstream. The unmodified AF-B1 undergoes several other biochemical transformations, of which hydroxylation results in the formation of a highly toxic 4-hydroxylated derivative. This metabolite is generated in the liver and is excreted through the mammary glands, commonly known as “milk toxin” or AF-M1 (Galvano et al., 1996). Aflatoxins are extremely stable chemical compounds and the effects of processing have very little to no effect on them (Jaiswal et al., 2018). Subjecting AF-M1 loaded milk samples to pasteurization, sterilization and other heat treatments indicated some reduction in toxicity; however complete degradation has not yet been reported. Some of the human implications of AF-M1 include (i) acute aflatoxicosis (highly cytotoxic species) and/or (ii) long-term issues caused by inducing gene-mutations and chromosomal anomalies (Kuiper-Goodman, 1990). Therefore, in light of consumer health and well-being, food safety regulations have been implemented in developed and developing countries to monitor AF-M1 levels.

The Codex Alimentarius commission of international food standards in association with the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has taken regulatory measures to establish maximum levels of contaminants in food and feed. Taking into account its toxicokinetics, rate of metabolism and absorption, the maximum level of AF-M1 in milk has been set to 0.5 µg/kg. The maximum levels set by the European Union (EC Decision 657/2002 and Regulation 401/2006) and Australia for AF-M1 in infant formula is 0.025 µg/kg, while in USA and India, it is 0.5 µg/kg (EC 401:2006). The first step towards ensuring public safety is the detection of contaminants, for which several chromatographic and immunological techniques are available. The liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LCMS) method is one of the most widely employed detection techniques because of its specificity and precision. For instance, Campone et.al, 2016 devised a fully automated ultra-high pressure liquid chromatography system tandem mass-spectrometry (UHPLC-MS) system to analyze AF-M1 in milk samples. The method limit of detection (LOD) for cow and goat milk containing AF-M1 was 0.6 ng/kg. Milk products such as yogurt were also tested and a LOD of 0.5 ng/kg was observed (Campone et al., 2016). Despite the evident advantages of sensitivity and accuracy of LCMS, longer assay time and the equipment set-up and maintenance costs have led researchers to look for alternative testing methods. To overcome the use of expensive equipment, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), a more portable technique was developed which was soon recognized as the “gold- standard” of immunological detection techniques. Known for its low operational cost, sensitivity, and selectivity, the ELISA has been developed and perfected in the detection of AF-M1 in milk and other complex food matrices such as wine, infant formulae, and yogurt (Jiang et al., 2013). An ideal competitive ELISA kit requires up to an hour of incubation time with a LOD of 0.1 ppb for milk samples. However, ELISA-based detection does come with a few shortcomings such as long sample processing time and the use of animals for harvesting antibodies and expensive reagents.

Aptamers have drawn the attention of researchers, as a viable alternative to immunological assays due to their ability to be synthesized in a lab at a lower cost, while eliminating the requirement of harvesting antibodies from animal sources. Structurally, they are a short sequence of 24–30 base pair nucleotides, that display high affinity towards the target (epitope or toxin), known by its dissociation constant (Kd) (Chambers et al., 2008). The aptamer’s ability to modify its architecture to that of the target epitope by establishing electrostatic interactions or H-bonds happens to be one of their most attractive features. With developments in nanotechnology, the synthesis of functional nanomaterials with enhanced structural and functional properties has been applied to novel biosensing techniques. Preceded by the discovery of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and fullerenes in the late 1900 s, zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials have captured the interest of scientists worldwide due to their extremely high surface-to-volume ratio and negligible size (Facure et al., 2020). 0D carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g.: carbon quantum dots, fullerenes, graphene quantum dots, etc.) are easy to prepare in bulk, are highly biocompatible, and are less toxic. Their average size of less than 100 nm, allows them to form conjugates and their unique photoluminescence property makes them an encouraging contender for nano- biosensing applications. Therefore, this study utilizes a graphene quantum dot-gold nanocomposite (GQDA) aptamer-based sensing system, tailored to the detection of AF-M1 in milk. The convergence of nanotechnology with microfluidics is a developing field and the application of 0D materials has been widely applied to The developed quantum dot-Au nanocomposite aptamer sensor was characterized using electron microscopy and electrochemical testing to assess its performance.

d-Glucose (C6H12O6) and 70 % ethanol were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Massachusetts, USA). Tri- sodium citrate di-hydrate and hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), potassium hexacyanoferrite (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium hexacyanoferrate (K4[Fe(CN)6]), hen egg-white lysozyme, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) tablets were purchased from Millipore Sigma (Oakville, ON, Canada). Okadaic Acid (C44H68O13), Brevetoxin- PbTx-2 (C50H70O14) were obtained from AbCam (Toronto, Canada). Tris EDTA and phosphate buffer saline solutions were prepared using Milli- Q water (18.2MΩ, DI water); as required. Screen printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) having a 4 mm working electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode, were ordered from Orion High Technologies (Madrid, Spain). To fabricate the master mold, SU-8 Photoresist (SU-8 2025) and developer solution were sourced from Microchem (Texas, USA). PDMS (Sylgard 184) and a curing agent were purchased from Dow Corning (Canada). The 21-mer oligonucleotide sequence used in this study was adapted from previous research (Kasoju et al., 2020).
5′- ThioMC6-D/AC TGC TAG AGA TTT TCC ACA T- 3′.

Thiol modifications to 5′ end of the above sequence were preferred for ease of attachment to GQDA was ordered from IDT Technologies (USA). Aflatoxin-M1 Elisa Kit (E-TO-E007) was obtained from Elabscience® (USA) to evaluate the performance of the prepared electrochemical biosensor.

The GQDs were prepared using a simple microwave-assisted- hydrothermal reaction, adapted from Luo et.al , 2015 (Luo et al., 2015). A schematic diagram of this process is given in Fig S2. Briefly, a 0.5 M solution of d-glucose was prepared in Milli-Q water and used as a carbon source for this experiment. The solution was irradiated for a period ranging from 3.5 to 5 min using an 800 W domestic microwave. The formation of a sticky caramel-like substance at the bottom of the Erlenmeyer flask was an indication of the successful formation of GQDs. In order to harvest the GQD, 0.01 M of tri- sodium citrate was added drop-wise while being continuously shaken by hand. The GQDs were let cool to room temperature and stored for later characterization at 4℃ in a tinted glass vial. The preparation of GQDA was achieved by the addition of 5 mM of HAuCl4·3H2O solution. The color change to pink due to the reduction of HAuCl4 was an indication of successful GQDA formation. The GQDA solution was also stored separately, in a tinted glass vial and stored at 4 °C for later use.

A 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- the solution was first applied to the bare working electrode (WE) of the SPCE and cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed. Once the basal electrode potential and the working window was set up, GQD and GQDA were applied to the WE in succession. Studies in the past have used the drop- casting technique to achieve a coating of GQDA onto the WE. However, several drawbacks such as uneven/patchy coating, poor stability, and reproducibility have been observed in the past (Liu & Mandler, 2020). Hence, the current authors used electrodeposition of GQDA to prepare the electrode. About 100 µl of the prepared GQD or GQDA solution was dropped onto the electrode surface and 10 cycles were performed. Once complete, the modified electrodes were allowed to air dry, followed by electrochemical studies using the redox probe. The thiol- modified aptamer was re-constituted using a 1XTE buffer (pH 7.2 in RNAse free water) to obtain a stock concentration of 100 mM. This solution was further diluted to a 1 mM working solution of which 10 µl was added to the modified WE of the SPCE. The aptamer modified electrodes were incubated overnight in a moisture chamber at 4 °C. The unbound aptamers were removed by washing three times before the electrodes were used.

The microfluidic design used is this paper has been adapted from previous publications (Chand et al., 2018; Ramalingam et al., 2020). The microfluidic device was designed using AutoCad® and produced using a high-resolution photomask by CAD/Art Services, Inc., OR, USA. An even coating of SU-8 2025 was achieved on a clean Si wafer (∼30 µm thick). Once pre-baking was performed, the wafer was placed on a hot- plate for about 10 min at 95℃. Next UV-KUB2 was used to expose the photomask onto the Si wafer to obtain the pattern. SU-8 being a negative photoresist, polymerizes only the parts of the design that are exposed to UV. Post exposure baking was performed again and set aside to prepare the PDMS layers using soft lithography. Care was exercised to ensure the wafer was brought down to room temperature before pouring the PDMS mixture (10:1 w/w ratio of substate to curing agent). The PDMS mixture was degassed and allowed to harden by placing it in a hot air oven at 75℃ for three hours. The PDMS substrate was then removed from the mold, and cut to shape. The top PDMS layer contained the inlet, mixing and incubation chambers and the outlet. The dimension of the channels was about 80 µm wide whereas the inlet chamber to the WE of the SPCE had a diameter of 4 mm. Next, the GQDA modified SPCE was placed between the two PDMS layers appropriately to ensure no leakage and exposed to plasma (Harrick Plasma, PDC-32G, USA). After 40 s at full power, both layers were bonded to one another. Multiple biochips were fabricated using this technique to ensure process consistency and flow characteristics.

The AF-M1 aptamer- target interaction is the basis of the proposed electrochemical detection system. The electrochemical potentiostat used in this study was PalmSens® (PalmSens4, Netherlands) which was also equipped with an impedance analyzer. The time of interaction and the amount of aptamer immobilized on the WE of the GQDA modified SPCE was optimized prior to the experiment. Once the optimal interaction time and aptamer concentrations were reached, varying concentrations of AF-M1, ranging from 100 to 2000 pM were injected through the inlet and hooked onto the potentiostat. Next, a solution of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in TE buffer was used to perform further cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) experiments. The sensor end of the microfluidic chip was inserted into the SPE connector, which is in turn attached to the 2 mm, 3 electrode receptacles. The working parameters for electrodeposition were set from − 1.9 V to 1 V with a scan rate of 0.04 V/s. The CV working window were from − 1 V to 1 V with a scan rate of 0.02 V/s and a minimum of 3 scans/step. The DPV used an Epulse of 0.05 V and at a scan rate of 0.025 V/s. The current range for all operations were set to 10nA to avoid overloads. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies, the frequency was set to scan from 1 Hz to 0.05 MHz.

Partially skimmed (2 %) bovine milk samples were purchased from a local grocery store. A volume of 2 ml of milk was first transferred to microfuge tubes and defatted by centrifugation at 8,000 rpm for 5 min. The cream layer was removed and the above process was repeated on the skimmed milk samples. Once defatted, the samples were spiked with a known concentration of AF-M1 working solution. The control samples were spiked with TE buffer (0 ppb AF-M1). All samples were run in triplicates.

Luminescent GQDs are nanoparticles that are fragments of sp2 hybridized carbon and measure less than 20 nm in diameter. They are known to exhibit quantum size effects as their excitons are typically confined in all 3 dimensions (Chen et al., 2019). It is due to these quantum confinement effects that GQD are commonly known as a non-zero band gap material. However, by modifying their surface chemistry, the bandgap of GQDs can be tuned to different requirements. Alleviating the drawbacks of graphene, such as the formation of aggregates and uneven distribution, GQDs behave like a molecule and are easy to work with. Their remarkable physicochemical properties such as non-toxicity, stability, biocompatibility and photoluminescence are some of the reasons for their wide optoelectronic applicability. A quick single-step “bottom-up” technique was used to synthesize GQD and GQDAs (Facure et al., 2020). In comparison to top-down techniques, bottom-up routes are known to produce higher yield and fairly uniform size distribution. Hydrothermal methods using glucose as the carbon source have been explored by Tang et al. (2013) and Wadhwa et al. (2020). Further, the single-step reduction reaction of tri-sodium citrate resulted in the conjugation of Au nanoparticles, forming GQDAs.

The schematic representation of the fabricated biosensor is illustrated in (Fig. 1 (i)). Various nanoparticle characterization techniques were performed to confirm the formation of GQD and GQDAs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was first used the view the morphology of the prepared GQDs and the GQDA. Copper TEM grids were placed over 10 µl drops of both samples and allowed to adsorb for 2 min with excess absorbed using blotting paper. Even dispersion and the absence of obvious aggregates in GQD and GQDA samples indicated the successful formation of quantum dots, as represented in (Fig. 1(ii) a and b respectively). The inset in Fig. 1a and b provide a histogram of the quantum dot size distribution. Using image processing and graphing software (ImageJ and Origin), the average size of GQDs and GQDAs were calculated to be 2.8 nm and 9.04 nm respectively. The quick reduction of HAuCl4 to Au nanoparticles pointed toward the edge effects of GQD and the synergy established between both nanoparticles. The concentration of HAuCl4 can be considered the particle size controlling step as a higher concentration results in a larger AuNP size.

Similarly, a magnified image of the GQDAs shown in Fig. 1 (c) appear to form an inherent core/shell structure where GQDs form a passivating layer over Au nanoparticles. To shed light on the elemental make-up of the synthesized GQD and GQDA, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with accompanying electron dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed using a FEI Inspect S50.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the GQD sample contained a carbon content of 96.4 wt% and the remaining was mainly oxygen (1.9 wt%) and chlorine (1.8 wt%). Furthermore, characteristic gold peaks at 2.1 keV and 9.7 keV confirm the formation of GQDA in Fig. 2(a). Similar spectra were observed in many randomly selected locations of the electrode surface, indicating a uniform layer of Au nanoparticles.

Visible spectroscopy was performed on both GQD and GQDA samples by transferring a small amount into a 96- well plate. The spectrum revealed higher absorbance around 300 nm but no significant peak was observed in the visible range. This could be ascribed to the energy state transition in the CO bond from n → π* and resulting in the strong emission spectrum due to trapped excited states. However, as shown in Fig. 2 (c), the addition of Au nanoparticles to GQD did display its characteristic peak at 525 nm (Bacon et al., 2014). The distinctive color change from pale yellow to pink is indicative of the formation of Au complexes with GQDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy was run on both samples to further explore the photoluminescence property of quantum dots. The GQD FL spectrum is an effect of uniform surface states in the sp2 carbon cluster, in addition to the quantum confinement characteristics due to size effects. Fig. 2 (d) displays the 340 nm excitation spectra of the quantum dots where the intensity of GQDA is weaker in comparison to GQD. This could be as a result of photoinduced electron transfer between the Au cores and GQD shells (Gu et al., 2014).

To further understand the functional groups, present in the synthesized quantum dots, FTIR spectroscopy was performed with results shown in Fig. 2(e). Vibrational bands in GQD appear at 1386 cm−1, 1226 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1, where the 1386 cm−1 peak corresponded to the NO2 stretching, while 1640 cm−1 peak signifies strong CO stretching and CC aromatic bending. IR peaks that appeared in the GQDA composite were 1077 cm−1 and 1031 cm−1 which point towards CH in-plane vibration. The intensity of oxygen-containing functional groups in GQD were seen reduced, in the case of GQDA. This could be attributed to the skeletal vibration of aromatic rings while using tri-sodium citrate as a reducing agent in its hydrothermal synthesis (Hongxia et al., 2019). The Raman spectroscopy of the GQD and GQDA is shown in Fig. 2(f). A 785 nm source laser was run over 10 iterations. It illustrates the vibrational modes of GQD and GQDA, characterized by their D and G bands. The band intensities are an important parameter to consider as they are known to increase upon increasing GQD sizes. Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) peak positions was observed at 1370 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1 in both GQD and GQDA indicate that the defect phenomenon (D- band) is a consequence of quantum confinement effects while G- band is a reflection of edge effects. Further, the ratio of D and G band intensities (ID/IG) for the synthesized GQD and GQDA were calculated to be 0.4 and 1.1 respectively. The higher value observed in GQDA as compared to unmodified GQD denotes the partial reduction of GQD (Panyathip et al., 2021). This result can be used as a quantitative analysis to confirm the formation of GQD-Au complexes by the introduction of defects in graphene structures.

Electrochemical studies were performed on the synthesized quantum dots to evaluate the performance of the modified biosensor. The working electrode (WE) of the SPCE was modified by as described in section 2.3 and is shown in Fig. 3 . The quantum dot stock solution was carefully dropped onto the SPCE and the potential was swept between − 1.9 V–1 V over 10 cycles, using electrodeposition, as displayed in Fig. 3(a). The number of cycles were determined over multiple trials, followed by running CV studies. Fig. 3 (b) compares the redox behavior of the bare electrode against GQD and GQDA modified WEs using 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- as the redox pair. The bare electrode gave a cathodic and anodic peak current of 141µA and − 109.8 µA respectively. However, with the deposition of GQDA, the peak currents increased to 156.4 µA and − 136.75 µA respectively, indicating successful deposition. This single-step deposition was continued for all later experiments while storing the modified electrodes at 4℃. The next step was to evaluate the stability of the deposition against varying scan rates. Fig. 3 (c) displays the cyclic voltammograms of GQDA modified WE obtained by increasing the scan rate from 10 mV/s to 150 mV/s. The absence of extraneous peaks confirms that the quantum dots have well adhered to the working electrode with the absence of visible lift-off (Ashrafi et al., 2019). Next, EIS was performed on the various modifications of the WE to give us an accurate picture of the charge-transfer kinetics of the system. The corresponding Nyquist plots for (i) bare electrode (ii) GQD and (iii) GQDA are displayed in Fig. 3(d) where the bare electrode demonstrated the highest charge transfer resistance (Rct). Upon modification with GQDs, the Rct dropped drastically and GQDA indicated the highest electron mobility. This observation can be attributed to the symbiotic Au/GQD core–shell structure of the GQDAs, as observed from the TEM images. The Randles equivalent circuit [Rs(Cdl[Rct Zw])] was further developed and fitted over nine iterations. The solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), double layer capacitance (Cdl) and Warburg impedance (Zw) were calculated to be 481.6 Ω, 1.4E + 4 Ω, 1.23µF and 3.04Kσ. Upon completing a basic electrochemical evaluation of the GQDAs, the following step is to immobilize the aptamer specific to AF-M1.

The 5′ end of the aptamer (AF-M1 Apt) forms a sulfhydryl coordinate with Au in the Au/GQD composite, making the 3′ end available to bind. Additionally, it also reduces fabrication time and cost by avoiding the use of EDC-NHS cross-linking mechanism. Aptamer concentrations varying from 0.25 to 1.25 µM were introduced onto the WE and stored overnight at 6℃ before its differential pulse voltammograms were recorded Fig. 4 (a (i and ii)). The peak currents were extrapolated to plot the graph where its maximum was reached on the introduction of 1 µM concentration. 1.25 µM concentration, however, showed a reduction in peak current, probably due to steric hindrance caused due to low inter-aptamer distance. Successively, the interaction time increased from 0 to 30 min, in 10-minute increments to understand the aptamer binding kinetics to the target AF-M1. This optimization step intends to maximize peak current within the shortest assay time possible. A target concentration AF-M1 concentration of 1250 pM was introduced onto the WE of the GQDA-modified electrode. After three washing steps, results indicated an exponential decrease in peak current, with the least achieved at 30 min. Although the highest current was achieved at zero minutes of incubation, this can be attributed to improper Aptamer/GQDA binding. A 1 µM aptamer concentration and a 20-minute incubation period was maintained for the final microfluidic biochip fabrication.

The microfluidic system employed here uses PDMS as a flexible substrate to promote appropriate mixing and incubation. The corrugations in the channel increase turbidity while improving flow characteristics, thereby increasing overall sensitivity of the system. The bottom layer of the biochip is altered to fit in a commercially available SPCE which improves its portability manifold. They were carefully sealed to ensure no leaks occur and the redox indicator was collected between the two layers. Furthermore, PDMS being inert, biocompatible and bio-stable makes it a perfect candidate for real sample analysis in a wide range of biological/medical applications. The design of the biochip was adapted from the team’s earlier publication (Ramalingam et al., 2019). Fig. 4 (b) is a photograph of the fabricated microfluidic device, whose end containing the SPCE was connected to the receptacle on the PalmSens®. The tubing extensions are provided to both inlet wells, are in turn connected to an infusion pump with a fixed flow rate. To ensure no leakages occurred, the devices were stored in a cool, dry dust-free cabinet. The microfluidic chip was first primed by pumping in 1 ml of 1XTE buffer and observing its flow characteristics. Next, the modified SPCE was inserted into the cavity and DPV was performed by introducing aliquots of AF-M1 to the WE.

Aliquots in the range of 100–2000 pM were prepared freshly in 1XTE buffer and DPV was performed. The specific interaction of AF-M1 aptamer with the target resulted in the reduction of the overall capacitance of the working electrode. A reducing peak height indicated the presence of AF-M1 in the sample.

Fig. 4(c) represents the DPV obtained, showing the varying peak currents from increasing concentrations of AF-M1. The cathodic peak current reached its maximum strength at ∼ 24µA at Epa = 0.125 V at 0 pM AF-M1. It was further observed that with increasing concentrations of AF-M1, the current decreased to ∼ 0.25µA at the highest concentration tested. The peak currents were extrapolated to produce a linear- regression graph presented in Fig. 4(d). The regression equation was calculated to be y = -0.0114 × + 22.46 with an R2 = 0.989. The LOD of the GQDA- AF-M1 sensor was calculated to be 397.3 pM using the formula (3*σ)/slope. The LOD of the fabricated biochip is within the ML (permissible value) set by USA and Canada for AF-M1 in milk.

In order the assess the sensitivity of the sensor against real samples containing AF-M1, milk samples were prepared as earlier elaborated in section 2.6. Spiked (processed) milk samples of known AF-M1 concentration were introduced onto the sensor surface and DPV was run. Results displayed in Table 1 (a) show an average error margin between 2 and 8 %, indicating good sensor recovery.

Further, to ensure selectivity 500 pM of the interfering molecules were introduced into the microfluidic chip with the modified SPCE (Apt/Au/GQD). Okadaic acid and Brevotoxin were chosen because they are cyclic polyether molecules similar to AF-M1 also fall under the mycotoxin family. Bovine serum albumin a common constituent of milk, and lysozyme (hen-egg) were also introduced to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor. Upon addition of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, the peak currents were observed and plot in the form of a bar graph was generated and is shown in Fig. 5 . Results indicated that equal concentrations of all interfering molecules produced a current much higher than blank (0 pM AF- M1). No significant interference effects were observed.

To evaluate the capability of the sensor, the spiked milk samples were compared to ELISA measurements. This competitive ELISA system used a 96-well plate approach to quantitatively determine the presence of AF-M1 in milk samples. The samples were prepared following the manufacturer’s suggested procedure for milk pre-conditioning. Once the standard graph was constructed, the absorbance of the provided standard solution against that of the sample was calculated. The % recovery of the sensor was calculated using the formula (Weng & Neethirajan, 2018):
R e c o v e r y
%
=
( C
″
- C b ) / C s

Where,
C
″
- mean AF-M1 concentration in the spiked sample;
Cb
- mean AF-M1 concentration in the blank (0 ppb) and
Cs
- known concentration of AF-M1 spiked into the sample. A comparative table of the performances of the developed biosensor versus the ELISA kit has been displayed in Table1 (b). The results indicated that the LOD of the sensor and that of the ELISA kit were comparable. Further, performance of this microfluidic biochip against recently published works is illustrated in Table S2.

This research examined an Au/GQD-based microfluidic aptasensing device that can help in the accurate detection of AF-M1 in milk. The use of an electrochemical SPCE is robust, portable, and also low in fabrication cost. The SPCE’s functionalization of highly versatile 0D nanoparticles (GQDs) with Au has remarkably improved its electrical activity, in comparison to carbon working electrodes. The ability of the aptamer to interact specifically to AF-M1, rendering an overall LOD of 3.97 nM which is within the threshold ML defined. This microfluidic device uses a simple 1 ml syringe instead of a syringe pump, making it more portable, in addition to being able to dispense appropriate amounts of analyte. The requirement of an on-site assessment system that can provide rapid yet reliable results during a routine inspection is advantageous to food- safety professionals. A quick response time of 20 min makes this biochip a potential candidate as a point-of-care device in the dairy industry. Upon automation it can also be integrated as an in-line detection system in milking parlors and cooperatives.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely to express their gratitude to the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada for funding this study (Grant # RGPIN-2017-03975). The authors would also like to acknowledge the Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs - U of G Partnership for funding this study through their Highly Qualified Personnel Scholarship Program.
